statement.en: Characterisation of the peritoneal mesothelium and its role in the formation of post-surgical adhesions.
statement.summary: The peritoneum lines the abdominal cavity and visceral organs within; it consists of a monolayer of simple squamous epithelial cells (the mesothelium) supported by connective tissue. Following major abdominal surgeries, scars form in the peritoneum of 63%-97% of patients, giving rise to adhesions. These can cause lifelong ailments for which there are no effective therapies; surgery is the current gold standard treatment for established adhesions but carries the risk of additional adhesion generation and bowel injury. Additionally, adhesions are a financial burden to the NHS. A key mesothelial marker is the transcription factor Wilms’ tumour protein 1 (Wt1). Adult expression is seen in the mesothelial and gonadal tissues, and kidney podocytes, where it plays a role in tissue homeostasis. Th

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: This is a preprint (dated 2026-04-25, categorized as cs.AI despite biomedical content) describing empirical biological research on peritoneal mesothelium and post-surgical adhesions. The claims are contingent on experimental validation, peer review, and replication. The abstract is incomplete (cuts mid-sentence at 'Th'), indicating provisional/draft status. Biological mechanisms are not fully established theorems but evidence-based working models subject to refinement.
